name: Antihydrogen 1S-2S transition matches hydrogen (CPT test)
requirement: Any candidate framework must predict that antihydrogen and hydrogen have identical 1S-2S transition frequencies, as required by CPT. The ALPHA Collaboration measurement gives the H-bar 1S-2S frequency consistent with hydrogen at the ~2 x 10^-12 fractional level - the most precise direct CPT test on neutral antimatter.

regime.description: Antihydrogen atomic-scale physics; magnetically trapped at sub-Kelvin temperatures.

current_bound.value: f(H_bar) - f(H) consistent with zero at <2 kHz
current_bound.reference: Ahmadi et al. (ALPHA), 'Characterization of the 1S-2S transition in antihydrogen', Nature 557, 71 (2018)

what_it_excludes: Constrains CPT-violating couplings affecting bound-state QED in matter vs antimatter. SME analysis: bounds on (a_eff) and (b_eff) coefficients in the lepton and proton sectors at ~10^-19 GeV level. Future ALPHA-2 measurements aim to reach 10^-15 fractional precision, comparable to hydrogen.

parameterization.name: SME lepton and proton coefficients: (a_eff)^mu and (b_eff)^mu for the relevant species, plus CPT-conserving Lorentz-violating coefficients (c)^mu nu, (d)^mu nu.
parameterization.reference: Kostelecky and Russell, RMP 83, 11 (2011); Kostelecky and Vargas, PRD 92, 056002 (2015).
parameterization.summary: A CPT-violating SME term shifts the antihydrogen 1S-2S frequency relative to hydrogen by an amount proportional to specific (a_eff) coefficient combinations of the constituent particles.

procedural.bound_in_parameterization: Dominant SME lepton-sector coefficients bounded at |(a_eff)^mu| < 10^-19 GeV.
framework_inputs: Compute matter-antimatter splitting in any spectroscopic transition predicted by the framework, parameterized as CPT-violating SME coefficients.

primary_references.citation: Ahmadi et al. (ALPHA), 'Characterization of the 1S-2S transition in antihydrogen', Nature 557, 71 (2018)
Kostelecky and Vargas, 'Lorentz and CPT tests with hydrogen, antihydrogen, and related systems', PRD 92, 056002 (2015)
